title: Pregnancy itch and rash differential primer
summary: Use itch distribution and lesion type to separate important pregnancy skin conditions. Itch without a primary rash points toward cholestasis; periumbilical plaques or blisters point toward pemphigoid gestationis; stretch-mark-centered plaques with umbilical sparing fit PUPPP more closely.
symptomPatterns: Itching without rash raises cholestasis concern
Periumbilical itch and evolving blisters raise pemphigoid gestationis concern
Stretch-mark-centered rash with umbilical sparing suggests PUPPP
typicalTiming: Pemphigoid gestationis often second or third trimester or postpartum
dangerSignals: Nocturnal palms and soles itch without rash
differentiators: No primary rash favors cholestasis over inflammatory dermatoses
suggestedWorkup: Ask where the itch started and whether there are true blisters
Inspect palms, soles, abdomen, and stretch marks
Check liver tests and bile acids if there is itch without rash
Consider biopsy with direct immunofluorescence for blistering patterns
carePath: Pregnancy rash with blisters or significant diagnostic uncertainty should prompt specialist review
Isolated severe itch still needs prompt obstetric evaluation because the skin can look relatively normal in cholestasis
pregnancyImpact: The same complaint of 'itching' can represent either a mostly benign dermatosis or a condition with fetal implications

title: Pregnancy bleeding and abdominal pain triage
summary: Early pregnancy bleeding and pain should be treated carefully because miscarriage, ectopic pregnancy, and other urgent conditions can look similar at first. One-sided pain, shoulder pain, fainting, or heavy bleeding push the pattern toward emergency assessment.
symptomPatterns: One-sided pelvic pain, shoulder pain, or collapse suggest ectopic rupture
typicalTiming: Early pregnancy is the highest concern window for ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage
Any trimester bleeding still requires context-specific assessment
differentiators: Bleeding alone does not confirm miscarriage
Pain plus bleeding does not rule out ectopic pregnancy
Imaging and hCG trends are central to sorting the differential
suggestedWorkup: Vitals and CBC if symptoms are significant
carePath: Emergency evaluation is appropriate if rupture, shock, or heavy bleeding is possible
Same-day clinical assessment is appropriate for new pregnancy bleeding even when symptoms seem modest
pregnancyImpact: Rapid differentiation can be lifesaving in ectopic pregnancy

title: Pregnancy diagnostic workup primer
summary: Common pregnancy symptom patterns often narrow through structured context: gestational age, blood pressure, temperature, rash morphology, hydration status, bleeding amount, fetal movement, urinalysis, liver tests, and pregnancy-safe imaging.
symptomPatterns: Headache or vision change shifts attention to blood pressure disorders
Itch without rash shifts attention to liver testing
Blistering rash shifts attention to skin exam and possible biopsy
Vomiting with dehydration shifts attention to urine ketones and electrolytes
typicalTiming: Gestational age is one of the strongest context clues in pregnancy medicine
differentiators: The same symptom has different implications at 8 weeks versus 32 weeks
Basic structured vitals often separate routine from urgent patterns
suggestedWorkup: Urinalysis and urine culture when infection or proteinuria is possible
CBC, renal function, liver tests, bile acids, or glucose depending on pattern
Ultrasound and fetal monitoring when clinically appropriate
carePath: Use structured data to support triage, not to replace clinician assessment
pregnancyImpact: Strong intake structure improves safety and grounded differential generation

citations.title: What are the symptoms of preeclampsia, eclampsia, & HELLP syndrome?
Urinary Tract Infections in Pregnant Individuals

title: Pregnancy emergency red flags
summary: A symptom-checking tool should elevate certain pregnancy patterns immediately: severe headache with vision change, severe blood pressure elevation, chest pain, major breathing difficulty, seizure, heavy bleeding, shoulder pain with fainting, severe dehydration, high fever, one-sided leg swelling, and reduced fetal movement.
symptomPatterns: Chest symptoms or unexplained shortness of breath
High fever with flank pain or systemic illness
typicalTiming: Emergency patterns can occur in any trimester or postpartum
differentiators: These features should override routine reassurance from otherwise mild symptom descriptions
suggestedWorkup: Immediate clinician or emergency evaluation rather than delayed self-monitoring
carePath: The app should advise emergency services or obstetric triage when these patterns appear
pregnancyImpact: Fast recognition changes maternal and fetal outcomes
